I was effectively given a second job (no raise, of course) after my company laid off a ton of people and I am beyond overwhelmed, not sure what my next steps should be I work for a large US company with several sub companies, and we lost a ton of people due to layoffs this past month. To keep things simple, I'll say Team A for my area of the company and Team B for the separate area of the company.

At least 4 employees with my job title from Team B were let go two weeks ago. My team was not affected. There is now a TON of overflow from Team B and we're expected to just... do it?? No training, just assigned these projects with firm due dates and left to figure it out on our own. Although we work for the same parent company, EVERYTHING else is different. Different product, processes, information, people. Everything. We can't even access a lot of what we need to due to distinctly different firewalls. Of course, I still have my main job, which is very busy right now. It is so hard to split my brain across two companies, maintain my regular work, and try to "just do" the other job without having the time or training for it. 

After a little over two weeks of this intensity, I already feel like I'm crumbling beneath the pressure. Leadership doesn't seem to understand what the problem is. They're treating it as "just" more assignments rather than what it is - a second job. I went from having a steady but manageable amount of work where I still had time to get up, walk around, take a real lunch break during the day, and not have work bleed into my non-working hours. Now I'm glued to my laptop for the 8+ hours and just want to go straight to bed after I finish due to the mental exhaustion. 

I feel like I'm spiraling out and questioning if I can even handle this career field for the rest of my life. I've worked myself into a bit of a niche (Proposal Manager) and feel like I would need to take a few steps back in my career if I want to change it now. Before this, I was on track for a promotion next spring, approved by my manager. I really wanted that for my resume, but now it feels like nothing is guaranteed. 

I'm also still fairly early in my career (2020 grad; 3 years here, 2 years in sales enablement, 2 years at an immigration law firm), so I don't quite qualify for most mid level positions (almost always 5+ years) while 0-3 years of experience positions are at a considerably lower salary. That's another part of why I really wanted to stick around for the promotion and a couple years after that, before my job was flipped totally upside down. 

I've talked to other team members and I know they have already updated their resume and are actively looking for something new, so I don't know how much worse it'll get for me if they leave first. 

At this point, I'm tempted to just do a bad job. If I keep working at this intensity to get everything done on time and done well, management will just see the end result and that the layoffs were effective (same jobs done for less money) without knowing the misery behind it. Then again, am I just putting myself at risk of getting fired? 

I don't know what I should do. I'm so stressed. We just keep getting more work piled up while being told to figure it out. My manager has tried to stand up for us, but she's been told it is what it is, essentially. The only glimmer of hope that I have is that no one told the sales department of Team A that this was happening, and they can see that we've already fallen behind on our standard work, so there are some meetings coming up between Team A and Team B's sales directors to negotiate a better middle ground... not holding out too much hope for those though. 

Anyways... Any suggestions? Do I just stick it out? Deal with it? Become bad at my job? Try to leave at all costs?

9mo Unemployed and 180+ Job Applications Later - Can't Help But Think "What is Wrong With Me?" - I Want to Rant and Need Encouragement I got fired along with two others earlier this year. No write ups, no PIP, just me doing my job as a luxury home builder construction manager. Only thing the three of us did was tell the boss that the budgets for each of our construction projects (10 total) he gave us were showing overruns and none of us could make our budgets pencil out; even someone who had 20yrs experience. Even when I started there several years ago, I took over a project that was $400k in the hole before I even touched it. Now on new projects, each line item was over by $10-$30k bringing the overall budget over by $300k; not just on one job, on all 10 we had different amounts over budget before even a shovel hit the dirt. 

Couldn't even move money from another line item to cover this overage; all line items were over budget and the contingency already used up. I even asked to see the boss's estimating template to figure out how he got his numbers as maybe his numbers were outdated and did not reflect current costs. If it was accurate, there should have been no way we should have purchased these projects in the first place; I mean, he was in charge of the land acquisition of each project after all. Instead of showing us, we were just gaslit saying that it was our fault; nothing wrong with his template. I believe we were the scapegoat to save himself to the owner of the company. 

Really though, I was employee 19 when I started and ended up as the 5th longest lasting employee after a few years because the CEO likes to fire people or people quit due to mismanagement. Company now has a reputation of a high turn over rate like a revolving door. I knew what was happening that day and remained composed and calm as one by one we were called in and people were left in tears. Honestly, I wasn't super worried being fired at the time as I knew I did nothing wrong to deserve it and I have solid career history. I mean, not only do I have a Bachelor's degree, I have a Masters along with several certifications and over 10 years of experience; I should be able to find a job in no time....or so I thought.

The competition right now is fierce with so many project managers out of work due to the economy. I've opened up to applying to out of state, willing to move, applied for similar roles that my skills could transfer. I've had some interviews that went well, but ultimately they choose someone with a little more experience or closer to them if they are out of state. Most applications I am flat out ghosted and never hear back. Employers are able to be choosier and take their time, pay people less due to the saturated workforce. I really did not expect to have to apply for over 100 jobs and taking over 9 months. "They" always tell you to have an emergency savings amount to last you a minimum of 3-6months but they don't warn you how it feels when your savings gets close to being depleted, you feel one step closer to being homeless living out of your car. 

It makes me feel pathetic having moved up the ladder in my career being successful, buying my own house, and living my life to have it all crashing down due to one person's ego. After 4 months, I had to make a decision to rent out my home and move back in with my parents, the ultimate feeling of failure at my age. Now here I am, waking up each day in my childhood room and getting on the job boards, company websites, networking and applying for as many jobs as I could find. It's hard having the motivation, adjusting each cover letter, each resume for a job application to reflect my skills appropriately, while simultaneously reading an email notification popup on the bottom right of the screen of another job rejection chipping away my hope. While I'm thankful I still have parents I could come home to as I know there are many out there who don't have a support network, it still feels emotionally draining. 

While I have had an in person interview recently for a role I found on a company website with only 4 sentences for the job description, I had to hold back my pride, my emotional reaction of being told the role is an entry level position with the salary range of making 50% less than what I was making, barely enough to afford rent in the area, told there are no advancement opportunities. I was successful holding it all in, even on my 1.5hr drive home until I had to stop to fuel up my car when it decided to have an issue stranding me there for 4hrs in the dark until the tow truck arrived leaving me with a $1,500 repair bill. 

What did I do wrong? Will I ever be hired? How long will this last? I can't help feel worthless, hopeless, and unwanted.

Is It Worth It? I don't have a specific question... but I am curious what you all think about my situation. 

I (30F) work for a very niche publication. I don't want to say too much about it because it would be very easy to figure out which one it is. I've been here just over a year and am becoming extremely fed up with how the company is run. This is a national publication with a large print and digital following and a very well-known reputation in this specific industry. When I interviewed for the job, I was under the impression there was a large team of employees, like at previous magazines I have worked for. Turns out I am the only full-time employee, plus the owner. The 'team' I was told about are freelancers and one part-time employee. We work remotely. 

Over the past year I've learned that the owner is the cheapest asshole on the planet. He got lucky that this publication with an already established brand basically fell into his lap when he is not qualified at all to be running it. 

I come from a corporate background where there were standard benefits, policies, etc. This job has literally no policies or rules. He makes one up and then forgets about it the next day. Here are some examples: 

PTO: 

When I started, I was told I had 10 days off per year. Because I am the only person who can do my job, it's clear that I'm not really able to take a day off without logging into my computer to do the daily tasks. Even the owner doesn't know how to do them (literally just scheduling and writing blogs). I've tried to take time off, but if I don't answer my phone or emails immediately, I get screamed at. A few months ago, the part-time employee said she was going camping and wouldn't have access to wifi or cell service for a couple of days, and he said 'well you better find some cell service while you're out there so you can get work done. He thinks because he never takes a day off that we shouldn't either. For the past year I've worked every single day, including most weekends. 

I had the flu a few months ago and was really sick, like a 103-degree fever for a week sick. I sent him a message letting him know what was going on, he read it and did not respond. The next week, he called me, screaming at me about how bad my work had been the past week and said I was ruining his magazine. For the record, I did not do anything wrong. The things he thought I messed up were actually all things he messed up that I usually catch, but didn't because I was so sick. When I went through everything he told me, I messed up and pointed out they were all his mistakes, he denied it, and got even madder. He also said I didn't tell him I was sick.... 

, 
Benefits/Salary: 

I have very limited health insurance and that's it. 

I do have flexible work hours for the most part, but this has kind of just turned into being available whenever he needs me to be. I get texts and calls at all hours of the night and day with him telling me to do things. This includes texting me on Christmas Day and other holidays. I also get sent free things from brands every once in a while to write about and he considers that my 'bonuses'. 

My salary is 50k a year. 

I have many years of experience and a master's degree. I've been here a year and a few weeks and I've heard nothing about a raise. I'm sure he forgot when I started and doesn't even know it's been a year. Everywhere else I've worked, I at least received a standard raise for being there a certain amount of time. 

Business Practices: 

Basically, if this guy worked for a large company, he would get sued in a day. 

He makes gross comments (told the part-time employee she needed to wear a "sexy red fiery dress" for an event we went to), and is honestly just extremely rude and condescending. He's constantly calling very important people we work/write about dykes and other rude names. He also tells me I can't be frumpy looking at events and will actually have to try and put makeup on and look good. For the record, he pretty much only sees me on video calls where I'm obviously not going to be made up like I'm going to a party. I've also been working in this industry for a long time and am very professional and know how to appropriately dress at events. It's like he doesn't believe me or maybe just doesn't listen. 

When we travel, everything has to be the cheapest possible. This means he stays at a nice hotel and me and the part-time employee stay at a cheap hotel down the road... and share a room. I've been on tons of business trips for other companies and have NEVER had to share a room with a coworker. 

He is constantly saying one thing and the next day flip-flops and says something completely different. This is for everything, including how the business is run. Nothing gets written down or put into a contract. I signed a very brief contract when I first started. 

I supply my own computer and equipment. 

I have a management title, which is the biggest joke ever. First of all, who am I managing if I'm the only employee? He treats me like I am an intern and doesn't have any idea what I'm doing. He constantly gives talks and lectures with "advice" and thinks he's the most knowledgeable person on earth. Usually, everything he says is wrong. If I make suggestions, he gets mad and tells me I'm wrong without even listening to what I have to say. 

He is forcing me to do things the way he thinks it should be, which is hurting our performance overall. But because I'm the one doing it, it looks like it's my fault. If he would just trust me to do what he hired me to do, we would be doing a lot better. 

Why did I take the job? 

At my previous job, I had to drive over an hour one way every day, and there was not a lot of room for advancement. It also wasn't in the niche industry I want to work in. This job is remote and does offer a lot of flexibility as long as I'm 'on call' all the time. It's also in an industry that I am extremely passionate about and love working in. When considering taking this job, I thought that I wanted to climb to the top of this industry, and this is a stepping stone/foot in the door. It's a small industry and there are not a lot of job opportunities every day. That's why I haven't quit yet. Especially with how things are in the job market today... I can't quit until I have something else lined up. 

I am starting to really struggle with my boss and he is making me not enjoy the work anymore. I just don't know how much longer I can take his disgusting and rude comments and the rage he gets when he thinks I messed up but really he just forgot something or changed his mind without telling me. If I had a great salary and benefits, it would be easier to put up with but... 

The standard salary for my position in 'mainstream' industries is 80-90k. I knew I was taking a pay cut because I want to work in this specific industry. I thought it was worth it, but now I'm not so sure. This is honestly just a few of the issues I'm dealing with. He is one of the craziest and worst people I've ever met. 

Do you all think it's worth it?

I was fired yesterday I was fired yesterday. 

\Edited to add context\

Sorry if I come across as rambling. I'm very upset.

I don’t know what to do. I feel like my entire world is currently shattering. 

I left my perfect work from home job in June 2025 that I was with for almost 4 years. The company is going through a lot of restructuring and layoffs, I was afraid of losing my job, so I took a job in my small town at a law office. It was a drop in pay, but the attorney seemed amazing and she promised she would get me where I needed to be. During my interview I told her my plans of attending college to get my bachelors degree, she said with this job would come with a lot of downtime to be able to get my college work done. 

Fast forward to now, I had been with her for 8 months and everything was going great….until we had lunch around the holidays and I was excited to tell her I was starting the process finally to take college classes. The following Monday she came to me and told me how shocking it was to hear that and that she needed time to recover, but is happy for me. We had a long talk, and she also told me that she wants me to step back from Paralegal work (I JUST got my paralegal certificate to get a raise) and she wants me to focus on administration tasks. She wrote up a “legal assistant tasks” and I asked her if it’s because I’m not meeting expectations - she laughed and said “No, you’re doing great! I’m not good at delegating legal stuff so I can do that and this is just so you know what I want you to do.” 

I also asked her when I can expect a raise. My insurance through the market place raised to almost $1,200 for 2026 (I didn’t tell her this) 

She responded that within the next 6 months I should not expect a raise. After that, maybe but it won’t be substantial. 

 

Overall, we left the conversation on a good note. 

Since then, she has been cold, distant, and very direct. I thought it was in my head and she wa just being very direct, but it’s gotten increasingly worse. I just opened my email and she has emails waiting for me to see right away tomorrow morning that sa “Ally, I thought you’d take the initiative and do this. But you haven’t. Please get this done” and “this wasn’t done so I sent it. “ 

A lot of emails saying “moving forward you need to make sure this is happening” and these are things that are just simply not my fault. I do A LOT. I went into the legal field with no experience. I took the initiative to get my certificate, get my notary, update internal processes….up into this point I have gotten all positive feedback. This is all coming from out of the blue. She’s treating me like crap, snapping at me….and I’m just miserable. I can’t stop crying. She’s been like this for weeks and it’s now becoming very obvious of how she’s treating me. 

I don’t know what to do. I feel like a fool for leaving my job. I’m in a small rural town and would have to travel over an hour to work. The job market sucks. My husband makes good money, I think we would be okay with just his income but I don’t want a gap in my resume. 

And all of a sudden since I told her I want to go to school she’s been putting really weird tasks on me (grunt work) to take up my time….almost like she’s sabotaging me getting my degree. 

I don’t even know if she’s upset because of that. She’s just being….horrible. I could do 10 things correct and she will focus on one flaw and use that to lecture me.

We had a Christmas lunch on 12/18 where my boss gave me a bonus and told me how happy she was with me, this is when I told her an update on getting my degree (something we discussed during my interview) 

On 12/29 she said she wrote up a “Legal Assistant Task Sheet” and I asked her directly if this is because I’m missing anything, she said no, she just wants to put it in one place. We had an impromptu meeting and I asked her directly again if she has any feedback for me, she said “no you’re doing amazing!” 

1/5/26 - ONE WEEK LATER. Starts sending me corrective feedback emails, being cold and direct and very negative in the office. I requested her to sit down for a meeting with me today, to which she said “I already planned to sit you down for a meeting” 

When we met I brought her the task sheet, highlighting things I do, processes I invented since starting and things that need more collaboration on. Since starting 8 months ago, I have created forms, contracts, internal systems, Facebook page with a following, created a Google business page, etc. you name it, I did it. She chose to take the things I created and used them as a weapon to micromanage me. 

Anyway, we sat down and she said she is terminating me and said I’m “not proficient” in my role and “missing things” 

I asked her why I am just now hearing about this. I asked her why the shift overnight has happened. She said she didn’t realize how much I was missing until after she made the task sheet - but it was after the task list was created that she told me (multiple times) I was doing great. 

Her reason for firing me? Because I forgot to add the cause number to a couple files. I told her in the grand scheme of things - that is a small error and something she should bring to me so I can fix. Even the most perfect people make mistakes. Coming back from two back to back holidays and getting an influx of calls of people wanting divorces, I was busy last week. Busy as hell. I asked her more for examples and she said “just a lot of small things.” But could not give me ANY EXAMPLES. 

I’m angry. I’m hurt. I feel completely used. She already hired my replacement. I left my dream job of 4 years and took a chance of this snake of a person. 

I took a video of my termination. I know she’s going to try to fight my unemployment claim. I just….i don’t know what to do with myself. I can’t relax. I can’t stop crying. I feel like I’m at blame, that I’m incompetent even though I know I’m not the problem. I have worked so hard on my career, I feel like it’s all completely crumbled.

Never Thought I'd Be In This Position. I've worked for this small practice for 2 plus years. I worked 40 hour work weeks. I was the Assistant to the Audiologist who is the owner of the practice. I looked up to her and she always told me that I was the best Assistant she's ever had. We would talk about everything together. The only thing that I didn't like was my Manager who my Boss basically lets run the place. Come to find out she's toxic. I was warned by a previous Employee that she shouldn't be a Manager. I didn't take her seriously. One example, I just had a Baby 5 months ago and was forced to take my 3 weeks PTO for my very short 7 week maternity leave leaving me with no PTO/Sick Days for the rest of the year. I had to call in one day a few months ago to take my Baby to the Doctor where he was diagnosed with Bronchiolitis. My Husband wasn't able to take him. I ended up getting a Doctor's note. When I had texted my Manager the morning of, she said that I needed to try to come to work. I told her that my Baby was coughing horribly. She said that I have no PTO and will need to have someone else bring him to the Doctor. I told her that I'm his Mom and I'm taking him. It's odd, because she will just randomly shove it in my face that I have no PTO. Yes, I know this!! There's other things as well, but would make for such a long post. Well, I've secretly been looking for a different job. I began removing things like picture frames off my desk and deleted the picture of my family on the computer. I was offered a remote job with better pay and flexibility. I've heard positive things about this company. Yesterday, I spoke with my Manager and tried putting in my two week notice which then turned into a 20 minute conversation. She said that I needed to think about it before I do. She made me feel guilty, so I told her that I would think about it. I didn't want to hurt my Boss. Fast forward to this morning, I told my Manager that today will be me last day working. My Boss comes into the office and nonchalantly told me that she's going to miss me. That was it! She didn't even ask me why or didn't even try fighting for me to stay. I busted my butt for her. This goes to show that you're easily replaceable anywhere you work. Anyways, I need to do what's best for me, my family and my mental health. I just hope this new job is a good fit for me.

Started a new job and was fired two weeks later for being sick. When I say "sick" I mean I have no idea what was going on other than having a 107 degree fever on and off for about a week. I could barely move to go to the bathroom. I had asked to push my start date back one week and HR didn't see an issue. I tried to work the second week but on day 3 the guy training me, told me to go home and that we would "start fresh" the following Monday (I was coughing so bad I was throwing up). However, this cough and trying to get out the mucous was still making me throw up in the morning (even brushing my teeth was hard) so that following Monday, I had been late by an hour (because of the cough) but gave the guy training me a heads up.

I provided documentation from my doctor and for the past week everything seemed fine.

Until today when I was called into the trainers office. I grabbed my notebook and pencil because I thought maybe I would be training and taking notes.

The guy who has been training me was not my manager.

The guy who was supposed to be my manager was on vacation for two weeks (when I was supposed to start, and when I tried starting).

I have NEVER EVEN spoken to this man nor have met him until today.

Well I get to the office and what would be my manager was there with a sour look on his face. He didn't stand, didn't greet me back, didn't shake my hand and I thought "wow this guy is rude".

No, apparently he came back from vacation, didn't like the fact I was very ill and decided to fire me today.

That was the first and last time we only had spoken to one and other.

I had just bought all this stuff for my desk so I had to ask for a box to put it back in and obviously I had to be walked out during my walk of shame.

I liked the job because it was a new opportunity to learn a new field in manufacturing but to be honest, I never really trained because I was constantly forgot about (the trainer was always busy).

However, I still took initiative and spoke with other employees and did what I believe was an amazing job for not knowing the area of manufacturing.

Honestly, I rarely get sick and this was just out of the blue (my partner works at a hospital). No idea what I had but it was BAD.

They (trainer and HR) kept saying they understand and not to worry. I mean they heard and saw what I looked and sounded like ontop of what my body did.

Until my actual manger decided to just come back from vacation and randomly come into the office and fire me.

The worst part is, I'm finally starting to feel better between the coughing and hacking.

I can't believe I have to find another job.

Struggling to land first job as an 18yo aspiring software engineer. What should I do? Hey jobspeople,

I turned 18 a few months ago, and earlier this year I actually received 3 job offers through my personal website. I also had one hour-long interview that didn't really go anywhere :/ Since then, though, things have gone relatively quiet. For the past couple of months, I haven't received any offers, and my job search has been pretty futile as well.

I'm mainly targeting software/programming/web-related roles at the moment, considering my skillset and background. I've spent years building my own projects, learning independently, recently I've also been dabbling in academics with the help of a professor... but the problem is that I don't have formal professional experience - just a few one-off and unpaid/undocumented things except one academic paper which can be considered something more "official"... I'm also still finishing high school (graduating in about 4 months), and my plan is to pursue software engineering in university afterward.

Right now, I'm feeling stuck, scared, and overwhelmed.

Most listings seem to require full-time availability, years of experience, in-office time despite me living in another country, or qualifications/certifications I simply don't have (at least not yet). I can't work full-time due to academic commitments, and I don't have "official" experience because no one hires without experience. The current job market feels like a paradox: you need experience to get hired, but you need a job to gain experience.

My main concerns are that I don't have much to include on my CV beyond my skills, personal projects, a couple of olympiad achievements, etc. - just informal and/or self-reported things. I worry that whatever I have to show might seem unofficial or not substantial enough for a professional application, and, like, where does one even put personal projects or like olympiad results? I'm yet to find an official CV template that includes these things and I've heard that making your own is a bad bad idea.

I'm also unsure how to approach recruiters and speak about myself to them (take my first ever interview that I had for example: I was sweating bullets with a shaky voice, honestly), and I don't feel fully prepared to handle interviews effectively. On top of that, I'm also uncertain about what my next steps should be after graduation: should I just work and drop any plans of further education so at least I have the "full time availability" point covered? I don't know what to do.

If I can't find anything tech-related, I'll likely take a retail job this summer (and if I decide against university, then longer) just to have income, but I'd really like to build toward a software career as early as possible and to pursue education \^-\^

I'd really appreciate advice on:

* What should I include on my CV at this stage?
* How (if at all) should I present personal projects?
* How do I approach recruiters?
* What should I focus on in interviews for junior roles?
* Is it worth to pursue education right after highschool, or should I just go into labour market full-time immediately?
* Any general advice for landing a first (tech) job?
* ...anything else that stands out to you about my situation.

If you need more context about my background or projects, I'm happy to share. Thanks in advance :)

Did I consistently make my managers look bad? You don't have to read all the context, kinda long. It's maybe a vent more than advice, but I'd definitely like anyone's input from a manager's perspective as to what I should've done differently. I tried being polite, honest, and helpful through and through even when my managers were voices raised and all. I kept it professional and always wanted the best for everyone.

Worked at a mid-sized engineering company for 3 years, partially doing grunt operator work and then I moved up and started doing product repair/ analysis. I had like 10 different managers (and their managers changed, etc) in the span of a year and a half, company doing a lot of restructuring for whatever reason.

In my first role I got some heat for writing code on a computer in my area for helping me keep track of parts going into an oven, I was running an almost fully automated production line and kept it running smoothly enough that I had some free time. (They had a system that was supposed to alarm when the oven reached room temperature during some part of the temp cycle but the alarm randomly stopped working and the engineer that set it up no longer worked at the company and no one could figure out how it worked). So I essentially almost got fired for working in notepad on the computer and writing a GUI so I could keep track of all the jobs and when they were supposed to come out of the oven. They didn't find a virus or whatever but I still got essentially demoted to a smaller part of the process that was still somewhat automated. But then I got somewhat 'rescued' from this by being able to switch roles. I heard from various sources that many people throughout the company heard what happened.

My first manager when I switched roles was an engineer and it came about when he asked me if i wanted to work on his team in the analysis dept. I said sure because it would be more freedom (his words) than the production line. I was with him the longest and he recognized my skills so he said I would be able to do more programming/ design work eventually. He got replaced by another engineer as my manager and he nitpicked a lot, and threw out the window what my previous manager said.

Around the time of this manager change I designed and 3D printed fixtures to help my department get things done more quickly, and get things done we couldn't even do before, (wasn't told to, just saw a problem that no one else was looking at and fixed it). Designing parts mostly on my own time and with my own 3D printers. It wasn't specifically my job to do this which is why I did it in my spare time, but it's what I liked and what I thought would show I could save the company big money and time and maybe transition into a role like that.

So my second engineer boss said he's not going to say I can't design stuff at home and bring it in, but he can't OK it on company time, even if it does work and save company money and time. Sure. But then he said I couldn't design stuff on my lunch break at my computer at my desk, because people would see, even though no big wigs ever came by, just him... then he started nitpicking the way I was sitting, me going to the production line to make sure the rework I was doing was actually fixing the issues with the parts (our processes/ methods weren't accurate in solving issues). I learned (he passive aggressively told me on the prod floor) it was because he was comparing my output to my coworkers (that were using fixtures I designed to help increase their numbers, mind you), and my numbers were lower. For one, I thought we were a team, they were handling the easier/ lower cost items and I was fixing (and trying to prevent failure of!) higher value items, we had a good system going.

We had a new addition to the team so I showed him the easier stuff to work on so I had bandwidth to tackle more of the more complicated stuff I was doing (why my specific numbers probably dropped from a raw number not value perspective). He was repairing bare stainless steel housings which are way easier to do and lower cost (and I designed a fixture that made some failures an instant fix). The other coworker I had was the first in the dept and he essentially was the engineers go-to for retesting huge batches of parts that 'should pass' because they changed some testing parameters, so he was getting big output boost in many ways too. This essentially made me try to work harder to design stuff to save me time so I could keep up and earn my keep (not going to gatekeep something I designed from the team though lol).

I wasn't expecting him to thank me or anything but he never cared for me saving time and money, just told me to get back to work. Kinda ironic that an engineering director that everyone hated because he knew his shit and did not spare feelings one bit even mentioned to me that he appreciated the big $50k scrap reduction I did just designing one fixture in my own time (I don't think he knew that part though lol, probably thought I was an engineer or something). He was quickly outed, likely mostly because in a company wide meeting he said 'we only hire the best and if you're not the best you will be fired', many people were panicking with that.

They put a random engineer in his place that always rubbed me the wrong way, always some kind of condescending when HE didn't understand something that seemed pretty easy to understand mechanically. He also seemed to have made disparaging personal comments about one of my coworkers (before he became director), so that made me more annoyed by him. He was buddies with the nitpicky boss I had, so boss got moved up to manage engineers.

I got a new outside manager, older, former military (but not like a drill sergeant or anything I don't think), he did some repair work in medical previously. He came in saying he wasn't a micromanager and then very quickly started getting on everyone not just me, I still have contact with people there but I was the first to go. I was told that one person on the team (they merged teams at one point while I was there) put in a request and got transferred to a different part of the company, the person she was training to help her quit shortly after, her brother that got 'promoted' similar to me (smart guy, I trained him on the prod line and he replaced me when I got heat), essentially replaced me again, also is no longer working there. There was another person that left (or fired, who knows) a month after me. The ones that are left were the quiet ones and just went through the motions. He tried getting after a tech that has been there for 40 years over start time, he wanted him to go in exactly at his start time, not 15 before. But we assume manager ran to the director and he told him to back off of him because he's been there so long and is good at his job, he apologized the next day and told him to continue ignore what he said. Just random nonsense that didn't affect anything tangibly.

They were saying I wasn't supposed to be helping engineering, when my coworkers were, same title and everything. Monitoring the exact time WHEN I went to take unpaid break, and if I took it at 1:01 my manager was watching and would say something. I could come in any time up to 9:00 am (well new manager changed that, if I said my start time was 7:00 and I was late I'd be penalized, but if I said 9:00 and wasn't late I'd be good), so there was no real impact of when I started working and by extension when I took breaks, I wasn't a huge critical part of a process anywhere, no one would be needing me. There were a lot of contradictions by the new manager, he would tell me to do one thing and I'd do it and he get on me about it not remembering he told me to do it that way. One exchange went like:

\corners me in the plant\

Him: "What do we need to do to get you to stay at your desk" x5 aggressively. 
Me: "I'm getting glue from the tech cabinet so I can do-..." 
Him:"Who told you to do it that way??" 
Me:"You, remember-...." 
Him: " Well you should've told me so I could get it for you" 
Me: "Do you have the password to the tech cabinet" 
Him: "No... we're working on it" (yeah right) 
Me: "Okay, so can I go with this person that has the password (so I can do my job)?" 
Him:"Ok"

There were plenty of interactions like that. Let me do my job and save the company time and money. Why you gotta make it hard for people to do a good job. This reminds me of extra context, the new coworker I had that was tackling the easier work, we both started complying with our inaccurate process knowing we weren't fixing the parts but management only seemed to take into account output not including input, so it meant we'd have more work that would make us look better recurring every week. The work I was doing had way fewer of faulty parts recurring, and it was likely already too late to save me me even if I could move huge quantities by maliciously complying anyway. He's still there to this day though.

The day they fired me I had just figured out another huge problem with our PCBAs, that could've prevented a lot of scrap and wasted time for many different types of failures. Essentially the PCBA was causing lasers attached to it to die, it would take varying amounts of time (differed by bad board) for it to die but it would. Best part was that our current process was saying if a laser is dead it's the laser not the board, so we had so many bad boards in circulation that were causing failures and we didn't know it, and we were adding to it. They'd never leave circulation. So they'd be increasing over time if the process wasn't changed. My coworkers didn't believe it even after seeing it with their own eyes.

I will give credit to my last manager though because at one point he actually did listen to what I had to say, and had a brief window where he was able to communicate properly. I told him parts take different amounts of time to get done (have to figure out what's wrong) and I usually take the harder stuff because it has to get done too (which led me to figuring out hidden problems like I just mentioned). So he set up meetings with the team and we went over how long things should take and that he would prefer us to concentrate on the easier then harder repairs, which wasn't a bad method at all, I started using his method (it didn't matter ultimately, because what you get done in a week is what you get done in the week, no matter the order, but not going to try to explain that, so it's really about optics if someone was looking only at daily output). But then he started finding other things to nitpick even though I was listening to them and implementing what he wanted nitpick after nitpick. I put timers on all my breaks when I took them and forewent them if I was late to taking them so it wouldn't count against me. It sucked pretty bad at the end. I could never join the military if that's how they operate.

Onsite Work Depression These past few years have been a whirlwind. I graduated college in 2024 with a degree in Information Technology. I started a project management internship, got engaged, and got hired by a government organization. While waiting on my clearance I didn’t hear anything for a while. When I finally reached out, they told me the position didn’t exist anymore because of the new presidential administration. That one hurt.

I started working at my mom’s job, a private school, as an admin assistant. Then mid year I finally landed a Business Analyst position. Three days remote, two days on site. It was amazing. I’m in my early 20s and it felt like the start of my real adult life. I was learning what it actually takes to be a BA. I had energy. I had time to see friends, cook, try new hobbies, do chores, plan my wedding. I finally felt independent while building my career.

At the beginning of fall, our new CEO changed the policy to one day remote per week. His reasoning was that federal workers were going back, so we should too. Our union sued because that wasn’t a valid reason. We haven’t heard anything since.

I was nervous but I told myself people do this every day. I’ll adjust.

Four months in and I’m not fine. I feel completely depressed. I’ve had multiple panic attacks just thinking about having to go in. There are mornings I sit in my car trying to calm myself down before walking inside. I actually started therapy because of this. I’m trying to be proactive. I practice mindfulness. I try to reframe things. It helps a little, but the dread is still there.

Every day I sit in my fluorescent light cubicle, open my laptop, and scroll up and down to make it look like I’m working when I’m not. Everything we do is still online. There are days I go the entire day in office and don’t speak to a single person. It’s silent. Dozens of cubicles and you could hear a pin drop.

When someone says “Feels like a Monday” or “Almost Friday,” I physically roll my eyes. It feels so fake and repetitive. I’m in my early 20s surrounded mostly by people in their 50s and 60s. Even my boss said I am the youngest person in the company for my pay bracket. I don’t hate older people. I learn a lot from them. I just can’t relate. Most of my coworkers have kids older than me. Everyone is in a completely different phase of life.

And it’s not just the age gap. The culture here is so serious all the time. My company never prioritizes employee appreciation. Department meetings get cancelled or rushed because there is always some crisis. 1:1s with my boss usually get cancelled an hour before they happen. We didn’t even have a holiday party or gathering this year. No opportunity to casually connect with anyone. I’ve never done any team building. Even our mental health day got cancelled. The other office building a few minutes from ours had one and they brought in puppies. I was actually really looking forward to it. Ours got cancelled because of snow and there’s been no word about rescheduling.

Even if I did make work friends, I don’t know if we’d click. Not just because they’re older, but because everyone feels so serious. My interests are concerts, tattoos, traveling, random hobbies I pick up with my friends. It feels like two completely different worlds.

I once had a meeting with four people who sit in the cubicles around me. We were all on Teams. Why are we on Teams if we’re sitting next to each other? That’s what frustrates me the most. There is truly no reason to be there.

When I first started, I loved the trainings. My boss gave me YouTube videos and self paced courses. I’d get tasks here and there. If I finished my work, my downtime was my own. By the end of the day, I still had energy and time.

Now I get the same small amount of tasks, but during downtime I just have to sit there. I listen to music and podcasts quietly. I take professional courses on my laptop because I don’t want to do anything personal on it. I ask my boss every time I see her for more work. I explicitly say I don’t have enough on my plate. She’s overwhelmed and doesn’t have the bandwidth to delegate. I ask others if they need help but same thing.

So I sit there. With nothing to do. No one to talk to. For eight hours.

The commute, getting ready, meal prepping, all of it adds up. I did the math. If I worked remotely full time, I’d get about three hours of my life back, four extra days a week. That time is everything to me. I love trying new hobbies and new activities. Now by the time I get home, I do chores, work out, and it’s time to get ready for bed.

Most mornings when I’m driving, I hope for some inconvenience so I don’t have to go in. Even when I’m not at work, I’m thinking about having to go back. I went away for my birthday weekend and barely enjoyed it because Sunday was hanging over me.

To my fiancé, I literally call it torture time when I leave for work. He feels so bad and wants to fix it for me, but he can’t. That makes me feel even worse because from the outside it probably just looks like a normal job.

I’m starting to realize I might have a fear of being stuck. When I travel, I don’t panic that the plane will crash. I panic that I can’t get home if I need to. I feel it at appointments and waiting rooms too. That feeling of you have to be here and you can’t walk out even if you want to. Being in the office triggers that exact feeling.

Working from home feels like freedom. It means I can work from my couch, my desk, a coffee shop, anywhere. It’s not about being lazy. It’s about autonomy. When I was remote three days a week, my mental health was genuinely better. I was productive and I was happy.

I don’t hate the work. I actually enjoy being a BA. I like working hard when I get work. I just feel completely suffocated by the environment.

On paper, I’m doing well for my age. Degree. Stable job. Engaged. But I feel like I failed myself because I’m not happy.

I’ve been applying to remote roles nonstop. I’ve signed up for things like Meterwork and Rat Race Rebellion along with the regular ones. I tailor my resume. I reach out to recruiters and hiring managers. I try to avoid listings that look sketchy. Still nothing. I know the market is saturated, but the jobs exist. I’d even take a small pay cut at this point.

Should I maintain hope? How did you land your remote job? How do you avoid scams, fake listings, and recruiters with AI reviews? I've read most threads on landing remote jobs but if you have any advice not typically mentioned I am happy to hear it.

I don’t think I’m lazy. I think I’m just in the wrong environment. But I’m starting to feel weak for struggling with something so many people seem to handle every day.

Have you ever quit a job without a backup plan? How did it go? Some people describe it as the best decision they ever made because it basically saved their mental health; others say it was a massive mistake that led to months or years of stress, debt, and depression. 

One story was from someone whose job had completely wrecked their head. They were having constant anxiety, could barely sleep, and every Sunday night felt like a panic attack waiting to happen. They weren’t just “a bit stressed,” they were at the point of fantasizing about minor accidents just so they wouldn’t have to go into work. They didn’t have another offer lined up, just some savings and a very strong sense of “if I stay, something is going to break.” They quit, felt terrified for a few weeks, but then slowly started to feel human again: better sleep, more energy, less constant dread. After a couple of months they landed another job that paid roughly the same, and they said they didn’t regret quitting for a second, because in their mind it wasn’t a career move, it was self-preservation.

Another person shared pretty much the opposite outcome. They hated their job, had a horrible manager, and one day just snapped and resigned on the spot with no savings, no plan, and no idea how long it would take to find something else. For the first week they were euphoric, like “I finally got out.” Then rent was due, their bank balance kept shrinking, and the job market turned out to be slower than they expected. Temporary “freedom” turned into months of constant money stress and feeling like a failure. They eventually did find work again, but they called that period one of the darkest times in their life and said if they could go back, they would still aim to leave, just not by walking out without any financial cushion.

There was also a story from someone who left a high-paying job with no backup because the job was literally making them sick. They’d taken a new role for a big salary jump, but the workload and politics were brutal. They started having panic attacks, their blood pressure shot up, and their doctor told them bluntly that the job was doing real damage. They had some savings and a partner with a stable income, and one day they decided that no paycheck was worth ending up in the hospital. They quit with nothing lined up and spent the next couple of months doing part-time gigs, going to therapy, and slowly rebuilding their confidence. They admitted the financial hit hurt and they had a lot of guilt at first, but they also said staying would have destroyed their health, and they’d rather climb back up from a career setback than from a breakdown.

Another memorable one was from someone who did what I’d call a “planned no-backup quit.” They didn’t have a job offer, but they treated leaving like a mini-project: they calculated their expenses, saved enough to cover six to nine months of modest living, and set a clear rule for themselves: “After I quit, I’ll give myself one month to rest and decompress, then I’ll treat job hunting as my full-time job.” They quit, took that month to reset, then spent their weekdays sending applications, networking, and upskilling. They still had moments of panic—especially when they didn’t hear back from places—but because they’d set expectations and had money earmarked for that period, it never fully turned into chaos. A few months later they landed a role that fit them better, and they said the key difference wasn’t “luck,” it was that they didn’t jump with an empty tank.

Quitting without a backup isn’t automatically a good or bad move. It’s more like a trade: you’re swapping a known, painful situation for a chunk of uncertainty. For people who are already at the edge mentally and have at least some financial cushion or in-demand skills, that trade can be worth it and even life-saving. For people with no savings, a weak job market, or existing mental health struggles, that same move can make things much worse before they get better, if they get better at all. So if you’re thinking about it, the question isn’t just “Am I unhappy enough to leave?” but also “If I do what these people did, what resources and support do I actually have, and what’s the worst-case scenario I could realistically handle?”

Just got my dream job after 8 months of rejection here's what I learned about resumes Hey everyone,

I hope this helps someone going through what I went through. After getting rejected from 200+ applications, I finally landed my dream role last week and I'm still processing it.

For months I thought my skills weren't good enough, that I needed more certifications or experience. But honestly? It was my resume that was killing my chances. I know it sounds obvious now but back then I was too focused on applying everywhere instead of fixing the basics.

The turning point came when I started treating my job search like a business (I'm from Mumbai, btw). I realized successful companies don't just build great products - they present them well too. Same with resumes.

Here's what actually worked for me:

1. Resume formatting - I tried bunch of tools like Canva, Resume.io, and even ResumeFlex. ResumeFlex was surprisingly good for ATS formatting and didn't break the bank. The key was having different versions for different roles.

2. Finding the right companie - Used Apollo.io to research companies and find actual decision makers instead of just applying through job boards. Game changer honestly.

3. Professional email - Got a custom domain email instead of using gmail123@gmail.com. Sounds small but it made me look more serious.

4. Following up - Most people don't follow up. I did, respectfully, and it opened doors.

The emotional part? Man, those 8 months were rough. There were days I questioned everything. But looking back, each rejection taught me something. Your resume isn't just a document it's your first impression, your marketing material, your foot in the door.

If you're struggling right now, please don't give up. Fix your resume first before applying to 100 more places. Spend time on it. Get feedback. Test different versions.

You got this!

Offer after 9 months of unemployment. Hang in there! I was laid off from my job as a robotics engineer back in January. With 3 years of experience in a field that’s supposed to be “in demand,” I figured I’d land something new within a couple months. Thankfully, my partner’s income covered our essentials, but we had to cut out pretty much all the fun stuff we used to spend on. This put a lot of strain on our relationship. Job hunting quickly took over my brain. I was losing sleep, waking up grinding my teeth, and stuck in a constant cycle of anxiety.

Despite that, I was averaging 2–3 interviews a week while only applying to around 5 jobs weekly. This required scouring LinkedIn and Indeed for like 4 hours a day because of how niche my field is, so the pool was small, but the response rate was decent. (At the bottom I’ll share the AI prompt I used to relatively consistently land interviews, in case anyone else is struggling there.)

My biggest hurdle wasn’t my resume or experience, it was me. I get super nervous in interviews, which is the total opposite of how I normally am. Even when I knew the answers to technical or behavioral questions, I came across as unsure, which doesn’t inspire confidence in a potential teammate. (That being said, a handful of the roles I was rejected for like 9/8/7 months ago are still being reposted regularly on LinkedIn so maybe they're looking for unicorns?)

Earlier this week, I had my 10th final-round onsite interview. The next morning, I got the rejection email. I felt crushed and almost canceled another onsite scheduled for that same afternoon with a different company. Instead, I just said “fuck it” and went in with zero prep and an “I don’t care” mindset. Something flipped. I came across confident, nailed my presentation, and actually felt like myself again. Everyone I spoke to seemed genuinely excited about my skills and experience and wanted my thoughts on what direction to take a few projects.

Last night, I got a call from the recruiter with a verbal offer at the top of the salary range I gave them. I accepted on the spot. I start next week, and for the first time in a long time, I'm looking forward to complaining about having to go to work haha.

AI resume creation tips:
I've been using Google Gemini Pro 2.5. I've tried the paid versions of Claude and ChatGPT and for whatever reason my results between the models was night and day. I was getting interviews for maybe 10% of my applications with pro Claude and pro ChatGPT, and between like 30-50% with Gemini which is free.

My prompt:
"This is the job I want: [copy and paste JD]

I'll attach an old version of my resume I used for another role as the starting point. Help me ATS optimize my resume for this role while making me a strong applicant. I'd like the job bullet points to start with strong action verbs to showcase my impact. I'd like to keep it roughly the same length since im maxed out on space. I'd like the professional summary to be a cohesive narrative instead of a keyword term dump. I'd also like you to include anything a hiring manager would expect to see on this resume based on what im applying to and my past jobs.

Then return an ATS score out of 100 and give me a critical hiring manager opinion"

This usually spits out a resume that scores 90%+ on ATS that should be pretty true to whatever resume you put in. Just note that some of the “hiring manager concerns” it raises won’t fully apply, so pick the ones that feel most impactful to have Gemini implement.

Be careful not to stray too far from your honest skillset / experience because if you start mixing up details in interviews it comes across very bad (ask me how I know lol) 

Happy to answer any questions or share more details if it helps anyone.

7 interviews in 2 weeks. Here's what DID and DID NOT work for me. I've been in a weird stage of life where I'm sort of job hopping and sort of trying to go out on my on and start my own stuff. This basically has just turned into me being forced to job hop, land short term contracts, and freelance (even if I pay contractors below me).

While that's not really the point here, I figured I'd explain why I'm looking for jobs consistently AND sharing what worked for me. 

For proof/context (happy to share screenshots) two of the jobs I was offered was full time, both in the 6-figures range. Another that gave me an offer was a contract for $120 per hour for up to 12 months. I also managed to land a few others that paid less but were still in the $60 - $80 range, with another one being at $40/hr.

Just about everyone is looking for a job, or looking for a better job.. but most people also don't get what works. There's a lot of AI slop right now, tons of ATS filters, too many spam applications, and a lot of other garbage that makes it hard to stand out.

And while I don't know the solution to all of this, I do know what has worked for me, so I will share that.

The first one is customizing the resume per job opening.

I'm definitely not the first person to say this, but I do have a few extra points on this.

Even though a lot of people say this, I don't just mean swap the company name and change the order of accomplishments.

At one point I actually did freelance work for several recruiters and I got to learn a lot about their filtering systems. I found that mirroring the exact language from job descriptions and using specific experiences made a gigantic impact. 

So the moral of the story for this one: adapt the terminology and language per job application AND only choose relevant experiences OR make the experiences sound adapted for that particular position every time. 

Lots of AI tools can help with this step. Even if you have to lightly revise them each time it can still save you hours a day in application time. Instead of 4-6 a day I was doing around 30-50 a day.. that way you get the quality and the quantity.

Timing mattered way more than I initially expected.

When I first used to spam applications I would always shoot for job relevance rather than novelty or opening date.. but this was definitely not the right move.

For some of you this may already be obvious but for me it was not. I always thought they probably keep a position open for a certain amount of time and review all the applications within that time.

And while some may actually do that, many of them do not. They review applications as soon as they come in and fill a good fit as soon as they can, often while the opening is still up (sometimes they basically never take them down).

So I started tracking when I applied vs. when I heard back on a simple app that let me track the progress/stage of all of my applications.

Applications within 24-48 hours of posting had a way higher response rate. Anything after day 7+ days was basically useless unless the application count was really low. Set up alerts, check daily and move fast (another reason AI and preparation is helpful here).

Last point on this: I found all of these positions exclusively on LinkedIn and Indeed. I always filtered for "posted in the last 24 hours" and targeted keywords that aligned with my experiences well. I know there's lots of job boards out there and plenty of them probably do work better, but I still just used the basic two job boards.

The LinkedIn DM feels awkward but actually works

I'm a pretty introverted person, so this felt super awkward at first, but it was helpful. 

Reaching out directly to recruiters or hiring managers with a short, genuine message (like 2-3 sentences max) pulled my application out of the pile more than once.

One of my interviews literally started with "oh you're the one who messaged me." That's it. That was the advantage. I would just send it in the invite/connection message. 

One time, the initial position didn't work out, but they had another one that was relevant and they shared it with me directly before they even posted the job opening.

In case you don't do this a lot, if you're applying on LinkedIn, usually when you apply to the job you can see if you have anything in common with the people managing the opening. If no one comes up, you can also search the actual staff of the company to find relevant titles to reach out to.

Like I said in the title, I also want to mention what didn't work well

Mass applying with the same resume works bad. I did this for quite a while and would just lightly update it every few months. You will get interviews despite what people say (if your resume is decent) but it takes a lot longer and makes it much much harder.

Another point I found to be helpful is to apply to every LinkedIn quick apply you see that has any relevance and is also "recent" enough to apply to. They are almost meaningless as there are a lot of job posting tools that automatically put those out, so many of them will go unseen, but they literally take less than 20 seconds. 

One of those jobs I was offered was through a quick apply application. 

Another point is that job hopping is waaaay easier than waiting for the "perfect" job.

Taking something that was "good enough" while I kept looking turned out to be way smarter and easier while also making me a lot more money. Not only was I being paid while searching, I was also able to use my existing revenue to always negotiate for more pay based on my value/experience. 

Anyway, that's what worked for me (and what didn't). I'm sure there's lots of other stuff that also helps or that might work differently for different industries but in general I like the path of least resistance.. and this is by far the easiest method I've used to land jobs quickly.

If you have other tips I always welcome them in case it helps someone (including me). Best of luck and thanks for reading.l

It’s now 8:30PM and nobody reached out to me today at all. My first thought was that potentially they offered the job to their first choice and were waiting on a response before they rejected me just in case their first choice said no and they wanted to offer it to me then. My second thought is they’re engineers and just busy as hell and didn’t get enough time to make a decision yet.

Either way, I REALLY want this job. I don’t dream of work, but this is probably as close to a dream job as I’ve found in my search so far. So all this being said, should I follow up tomorrow and ask for an update? If so, how do I word it so that I seem passionate and not desperate (even though I’m kind of both lol). Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

Interviewer said they’d let me know their decision by EOD the next day, then the next day said they’d let me know by Monday morning, then ultimately never did. When should I follow up if I should follow up at all?

How I landed 3 jobs on LinkedIn without Premium. Many people still don’t really know how to use LinkedIn. It’s a brutal tool, but if you’re open-minded and willing to learn, you can absolutely get a job there.

Honestly, for me and several friends who speak English and Spanish, it’s very common to find good opportunities on LinkedIn. If you still don’t speak another language, download Duolingo, sit down for ten minutes a day, and learn something that can actually make you earn real money.

Don’t just look at the “Jobs” tab. Search for posts from smaller companies or recruiters who advertise through normal posts, because it’s much cheaper for them. That’s where a lot of great opportunities appear.

When I got my first fully remote job, a US based company in the Customer Service area, the job post was only four days old and had twenty four applications. I stood out easily. So focus on jobs that are less than three weeks old and have few applications. Those are the ones that really give you a chance.

Applying to forty or sixty random jobs a day where you only meet half the requirements, or to jobs that have been open for months with over one thousand applications, is a waste of time or pure luck. If you don’t meet most of the requirements, or if the job already has too many applicants, you’ll rarely even make it to the first screening.

When it comes to your LinkedIn profile, the first thing you should do is reformat and upgrade your resume. If you speak more than one language, save a bilingual version. Remember that your resume must be readable by artificial intelligence, because almost every company now uses automated systems to filter candidates. Start by removing everything that creates visual noise and gets you eliminated immediately: company logos, school logos, separators, icons, colors, symbols, photos, your home address, and even other social media links. Keep only your LinkedIn. Your resume needs to be a clean, direct document made of plain text. Remember that keywords matter more than design.

Now let’s talk about making your profile stand out strategically. Recruiters pay attention to clean and updated profiles without messy or outdated information. Everything must be coherent and easy to read. Use the right keywords for your area, keep a decent professional photo, and choose a cover image that matches what you do. Avoid anime or cartoon covers.

One thing that really helps to attract recruiters is linking your certifications directly on your profile. This changes everything. Take relevant courses for your field, even short or free ones from LinkedIn Learning. But if you want something stronger to truly stand out, Coursera courses are excellent. You can request up to ten financial aids on Coursera, which gives you up to a ninety percent discount, so you can take around ten high quality courses for free. That includes programs from Google, Microsoft, and other respected names in the market. This increases your visibility and shows that you are actively developing yourself.

Write an “About” section that shows who you are and what you are looking for. In that part, send your resume to ChatGPT and ask for help making your profile more attractive. That simple change made all the difference for me.

I say this with confidence because I lived it. Instead of desperately sending resumes everywhere just because some people on the internet say you need to apply to one hundred jobs a day, I stopped doing that. It drains your energy, exhausts you, and wastes your time.

One day I decided to update my profile, added my certifications, started new ones, began interacting on the platform, made connections, commented on posts from people in my field, and supported strangers. I organized everything and asked ChatGPT to read my resume and help me rewrite my “About” section.

Then the magic happened. I started getting direct messages from recruiters and headhunters asking about my availability. In the last month I received two invitations for different selection processes, passed both, and now I have three jobs. I’ll keep the US based one because it’s remote and flexible, and I’ll choose between the other two that literally just appeared for me.

This is not an exaggeration. I’m writing this because it truly made a difference in my life. LinkedIn can be a tiring and sometimes even toxic social network, but it’s powerful. In less than two years I got three jobs through it, and it wasn’t easy. The US based job required three interviews, one of them with my Indian manager whose accent was very hard to understand. The two most recent ones were long processes with nine interviews in total, four for one company and five for the other. There were video stages, interviews in other languages, and a lot of patience. Most people give up at those points. They don’t record the videos or even show up for the interviews. But if you think about it carefully, it’s worth trying with focus and consistency.

Good luck, and I truly hope this helps you see LinkedIn in a different way.

The Two-Speed Economy Is Back as Low-Income Americans Give Up Gains From today’s WSJ-

The Two-Speed Economy Is
Back as Low-Income Americans
Give Up Gains
High-earners and older Americans are faring better than ever, while fortunes are sliding again for low-wage and young workers
Jeanne Whalen 
Sept. 16, 2025 at 9:00 pm

For many others, momentum has stalled or reversed. The big wage
growth experienced by low-income workers during the pandemic
has petered out. Those workers are curbing their spending and in
some cases are struggling to find jobs. Unemployment for Black
Americans and many young people has jumped. Home prices and
rents have risen sharply, making housing increasingly unaffordable.

The divided fortunes of rich and poor in the U.S. may sound like an
old story. Yet in recent years, workers on the low end of the
spectrum began modestly narrowing the gap, as acute labor
shortages enabled them to switch jobs and bargain hard for better
wages.

Now the gulf is widening again. For much of the past few years,
wages for the bottom third of U.S. earners grew at a faster rate than
for the top third, Bank of America data show. But since the start of
the year, top earners have pulled far ahead.

“As the unemployment rate has slowly crept up, and job growth has
fallen more sharply, wage growth has moderated, but particularly
for low-wage workers,” said Arin Dube, an economics professor at
the University of Massachusetts, Amherst. “This is disappointing
news for those who were hoping the reversal in wage inequality
would be a more permanent feature of the American landscape.”

In August, annual wage and salary growth fell to 0.9% for the
bottom third, the smallest gain since 2016, the Bank of America data
show. The top third saw growth of 3.6% year over year, the most
since November 2021. That divergence was echoed in year-over-
year spending growth in August, with household spending rising just
0.3% for the low-income group and 2.2% for higher-income
households.

The cooling labor market is probably fueling much of that
divergence, said David Tinsley, senior economist at the Bank of
America Institute. Federal job data and Bank of America’s internal
figures suggest that the softening job market is affecting lower-
income households more than other groups, Tinsley said.

The booming stock portfolios of higher-income households are
probably also playing a role, providing higher-earners the
confidence to spend more, Tinsley added.

The top 10% of earners—households making about $250,000 a year
or more—account for more of the nation’s total spending than ever,
reaching 49.2% in the second quarter, compared with 45.7% a
decade ago, according to Moody’s Analytics.

Wealthy Americans’ spending power is continuing to buoy luxury
segments of some industries, including airlines and high-end
sneakers. Flights to international destinations and in premium
classes are still selling out, while domestic and main-cabin sales
have softened across airlines. United Airlines said premium cabin
revenue in the most recent quarter increased 5.6% “while the
economy cabin was negative.”

For Camelia Kuhnen, a finance professor at the University of North
Carolina at Chapel Hill, much of today’s divide relates to
homeownership. Older and more prosperous households that
owned homes before the pandemic are sitting on real estate that is
now 50% more valuable, she said, citing the S&P CoreLogic Case-
Shiller National Home Price Index.

“I think the U.S. population is split into these two types—the lucky
ones, the asset owners, and the unlucky ones,” Kuhnen said. The
latter “are now stuck because there’s no way they can come up with
that down payment to buy a home.”

The median age of first-time home buyers increased to 38 last year
from 35 in 2023, a record high, according to the National
Association of Realtors.

At the same time, a booming stock market and robust economies in
tech and finance are generating expansive wealth and minting new
millionaires and billionaires. The divide is creating parallel realities of
American life.

The split screen is on view in the Chicago area, where wealthy
residents so far this year have already bought more homes at or
above $4 million than they did in all of 2024, according to an annual
tally by Crain’s Chicago Business. Much of the action is on the North
Shore, a wealthy suburban enclave stretching along Lake Michigan.

“We thought Covid was crazy, this is Covid times 10—it just
continues to take off,” said Jena Radnay, a real-estate agent in the
area who recently sold a $31 million French Revival mansion with a
private beach. “When [buyers] look at their portfolio, I think they
feel more confident they can take more risk. If you see your portfolio
going up 25%, you are feeling better about making that purchase.”
A mansion in Winnetka, Ill., recently sold for $31 million.

Alfred Baah, a Chicago cabdriver who immigrated from Ghana two
decades ago, shows the other side of the coin. He rents an
apartment with his wife and two children on the city’s north side.
The 40-year-old would like to buy a home, but prices are too high,
and he hasn’t been able to save money lately. His income has
dropped significantly this year amid a slowdown in his customer
traffic.

When the economy recovered after Covid, Baah could usually count
on picking up a ride at the airport without a long wait, he said. In
recent months he has been waiting longer—often more than an hour.
In a good year he might make $80,000, but this year he is on track
to earn about half that, he said. Meanwhile his grocery bill and other
expenses have ballooned. “Whatever I make is just to pay the bills,
and that’s it. I can’t save any this year,” Baah said.

Young people are experiencing a particular fall in fortune. While the
overall unemployment rate rose to 4.3% in August, the rate is much
higher for recent college graduates—6.5% over the 12 months
ending in August. That is about the highest level in a decade,
excluding the pandemic unemployment spike.

That rate, based on data from the Labor Department, applies to
people ages 20 to 24 looking for work who have at least a bachelor’s
degree. Economists say AI is to blame in some cases, because tools
such as ChatGPT can now automate work previously done by
relatively inexperienced workers.

Bleak employment prospects have helped tank young Americans’
views on the economy, to levels hardly seen since a prominent
monthly survey began in the 1970s. Typically, young Americans
ages 18 to 34 are the most optimistic about the future of the
economy in the University of Michigan’s monthly survey of
consumer sentiment. But since the start of the year, they are
expressing more pessimism than people 55 and older.

“This is extremely rare,” said Kuhnen, the UNC professor. “They
don’t have a home, they don’t have a large investment in their
401(k) and they are the most concerned about losing their job
should we hit a downturn.”

Not all data points to bleak times for groups who tend to earn lower
incomes.
Hispanic unemployment in August, at 5.3%, was a little lower than a
year ago, though it ticked up from July levels.

The trends are more worrying for Black workers, whose
unemployment leapt to 7.5% in August, from 6.1% a year earlier.
Historically, Black workers have been more likely to hold low-skill
and junior-level jobs than their white counterparts, making them
more vulnerable to layoffs. They have long faced discrimination in
the labor market that can become more pronounced when overall
hiring slows.

Federal job cuts may also be playing a role in a recent increase in
unemployment among Black college graduates. The federal
workforce has a disproportionate share of Black workers.

Write to Jeanne Whalen at Jeanne.Whalen@wsj.com
